speaker: interviewer
text: We want to now talk about America-- America, the United States. So we'll pick--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll tap you in a second. We're still recording the bars.

words.text: I'll tap you in a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Approaching her--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We were talking about your-- about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I was already-- yeah, I was already in America, surely. When I noticed it, there's no stay for him. Although, he got his pension-- or his-- they later cut his pension, too. But he has to be with us. He has come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I approached this aunt. Didn't hear from her, no answer. She was-- no answer from her. So I couldn't do anything for my father. And my brother couldn't do anything. And he was taken from Vienna, from his room. He was taken in the cattle wagon to Lithuania.

speaker: interviewee
text: That, with the mother of a friend of mine, they were together, taken. And as much as I could-- they went to the transport to Lithuania. You know, this organization, this Nazi organization, you want to kill a guy, kill him right away, in Vienna. No, they have to organize a train, take him to Lithuania. Now, the story, some say he froze to death in the track-- in the wagon. Other story is that when they stepped out in Lithuania, there was a machine gun in the chest. I tried to get him from the Red Cross. And I could not get anything, how he really died, and when, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrive in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I come to America. Still, with the help of the HIAS. They gave us a certain amount every week. They provided us with a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was where? Where, in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- Oh, yeah. When we came to New York, I stayed three months in New York, until it was decided where to go, what to do with me. That gives me three months, and I spent it in the Cooper Library, to learn Yiddish. If you know German, it's easier to learn Yiddish. And I knew the Hebrew. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And after three months, where did you go-- after the three months in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So then, you see the HIAS distributed the Jews. They didn't want too many stay in New York and overload certain cities. So they said-- first they asked me, where do you want to go? And they gave me the Cooper Library. I studied America in and out. I knew everything about the geography and where I wanted to go. And I said, I want to go to Oregon, Washington state, or Colorado, because there are mountains and there are meadows and there are forests. You see, I'm coming from the Alps. I lived in the Alps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, no doing, you cannot go there. But they said, we'll send you to Salt Lake City. Salt Lake City? How will I fit there? They are nice people, the Mormons, but it's not the place for me. OK, we'll send you to Bakersfield. And I knew what's going on in Bakers-- they couldn't tell me-- Bakersfield. What did I do as an engineer? What, in Bakersfield?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they said, that is it. You have to go to Los Angeles. Southern California, it's not the ideal place for me-- Northern California, would prefer Northern California, but the southern. So they sent me to Los Angeles. I didn't like it. My wife loved it. My wife loved it. So that was a good reason to stay here. And again, HIAS provided a job for me-- in so far that they were-- the Jewish industrialists, they gave a list when they would need somebody there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got my first-- no, I myself found my first job. But he told me, for six weeks only. And he was a structural engineer. And he hired me as a draftsman. I knew how to make plans, but I didn't know the nice lettering. So he taught me how to make them. He was an Irishman. [INAUDIBLE] his name was. Then, I got the job at Acme Tank. Acme Tank. It's Levine-- Levine's son-- his son was a congressman, Levine. Mel Levine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right. I knew him when his mother carried him in her body. They were very nice people. There, I got the first job. And Mr. Levine, Sr., he was very nice. I asked him for an affidavit for my sister-in-law, who was in Belsen. He couldn't give me an affidavit, but I got it later on from somebody else. Yeah. So being in Los Angeles now, having a job with Acme Tank, and then getting investigated by the FBI if I'm good enough to take to be in the defense work. So I was a structural engineer in the defense work and made a good living. However, then I started with the-- I took a job with the state of California, where I stayed for 20 years and got my pension there. But there I had trouble. There I had trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a structural engineer, with the state?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the state-- with the state.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trouble was disappointment-- disappointment. You know that I came with big expectation, with big joy to be in the country of the Bill of Rights. And there was Eisenhower, who proclaimed December 15 as the Bill of Rights Day. Ah, that is something. I took the Bill of Rights-- I got it from a bank, a picture-- and put it on the bulletin board, because I felt, always, the American people, they don't know how happy they are. They should celebrate it. I celebrated in my house, with the children, the Bill of Rights. So I put it up. And there we had-- in the State Building, in the Division of Architecture-- we had a retired admiral. And the retired admiral, he storms out when he the sees the Bill of Rights and tears it down and throws it in the paper basket. And I was shocked. But I was afraid. I kept quiet. Next year-- next year came, I put it up again. And this time, he doesn't tear it down. But he goes to my superior and tells him what Fred Buch is doing. And the superior calls me in. And he says, the Bill of Rights is controversial.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there is as little space for the Bill of Rights in this office as there is a space for foreigners who do not know the American way. That means he's going to fire me. What shall I do? By that time, I was already a member of the ACLU-- you know AC-- Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Had some encouragement from the ACLU. And I wired to the Governor Knight and asked him, this is Bill of Rights day. Is it all right to have the Bill of Rights on the bulletin board? And the answers, it's not only for the one day, it is all right to have it for the whole year. And I take the telegram, and I go to my boss, and I show him that. And he said, I don't care what the governor says. The Bill of Rights is controversial, and it will not be on the bulletin board. That means he still is going to fire me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, do you do you remember Drew Pearson? Drew Pearson, yeah. So I wrote a letter to Drew Pearson and told him the story. And his first step was going to the FBI to find out if Fred Buch was a communist or not. So he found out Fred Buch is not a communist.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went over the radio-- he had hundreds of radio stations-- and he blurted the whole story out. All in his columns-- he had many columns-- he blurted out on all of the columns. I got so many letters from people of America, all supporting me.

speaker: interviewer
text: One even gave me all his-- all his devaluated stocks. He said to me, you may have it, and try to sell it. Whatever you get, keep it. People were very enthusiastic about it. So yeah, what happened then? Embarrassment for the government. So the governor sent down the head architect. The first thing, he fired the admiral. And the second thing, he spoke to my boss. I don't know what he told him. But my boss didn't talk to me for four years. But after four years, suddenly he comes to me and says, Fred let's shake hands. It took me a little time to find out that you were right. And that was because I was devastated. If the Bill of Rights is controversial, I have no business in this country. I'm going away. It was my dream. And then, they tell me it is all a lie? It's only printed? So you spent your whole career with the state? And then you retired from the state?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the state and I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- shall I tell you? I really was working only 20 years with the state. And I took 24-years pension for 20 years. It was in 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I retired. And I was very happy there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Lord willing, your next birthday will be your 94th?

speaker: interviewee
text: My next birthday will be here in a few days, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Finally, I must ask you one more question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were a youngster, you came from an assimilated family. There was not much religion in your family. Today, as we sit here, do you believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, there is no proof for it. I have no proof of it. But I respect the Jewish philosophy, the Jewish ethical-- I'm happy that I come from-- I'm not proud, because pride is one of the seven-- I believe it is one of the seven deadly sins. I say always, I shall be proud of my sons. I'm happy with my sons. They are all very valuable. The sons are the very valuable contribution I gave to this country. They're good people. They're good people, and their contribution-- intellectual contribution is very valuable. And they are all a self-made men. It took us a little time to have roots in his country. And the kids, they didn't get a red penny from their parents for their education. They got scholarships, and they loans, partially, too. Although, it was not so terrible at that time, the education. But they are self-made. They were working. The physician, he was a cab driver, and he was a carpet cleaner. And the electronic engineer was a hamburger. And the youngest, he's a dentist. He also made his own money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Buch, you indicated that you have some pictures that you would like to show us, some memorabilia.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the camp, unfortunately. I have the other pictures of-- that would only be private pictures, or family pictures. But they are of no interest, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you do have pictures of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Shall I get up an-- this is me, the second from the left, Fred Buch. And it was made in the prison camp of Les Milles in Southern France. And it was around maybe 1930-- yeah, in the fall of 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Buch, how come you were taking a picture-- they allowed you to take pictures in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I smuggled it-- my camera-- my wife brought it to me, and I smuggled it in. And I was able to make these pictures, also from the catacombs. This is called the catacombs, which was really a brick factory-- the oven of the brick factory. There we were-- we lived there. As I see here, somebody put even straw in the bag. But we really had only straw on the ground-- quite comfortable, nevertheless. And this was the catacombs, it is called. And that is in the end of 1938, I believe-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was taken with the same--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Les Milles, the same prison camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Buch, this was taken with the same camera that you smuggled in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's the same location-- it's the same location. I have another picture of a writer. This is my brother, Dr. Ernest Buch. He was a physician and Kankakee, in the hospital there. Unfortunately, he died of emphysema and heart attack. At the time, they still didn't know the detrimental effect of tobacco. He was a heavy smoker, not knowing how deadly smoke is. At this state, maybe he is 50-- no, 57, 58-- 58 years old. The means-- 58-- that was in 1960, probably-- a picture of 1960. And that is in Kankakee, somewhere. These are my three children with their mother, a lovely woman. She is, here-- that might be in 1950-- 1950. The youngest is Tom, now a dentist, on the right side of the mother. Then, above is standing, is John, now an MD. He is seven years older than his brother. Yeah, he's 12 years old, maybe. And then it's Peter, now a retired electric engineer. But really, he was with the state of California running the computer in Berkley University. The date might be 1950. And the place is in our home in Los Angeles. This is my wife, Susie-- really Marian Buch. We called her Susie-- a lovely woman, very smart, very intelligent, and a good mother. She gave me three nice sons. That is-- yeah-- in Los Angeles, and the date is the year 1978-- maybe-- yeah, at the beginning of 1978--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Buch--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Buch, when did your wife pass away? When did your wife pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 16, 1978.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: 72. 72. And a healthy woman, only from neglected tooth-- bacterias from her tooth-- took a hold on her heart. And it would cause a heart disease. The motor function of the valves, which were destroyed by the bacterias of her neglected tooth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she ill a long time? Was she sick for a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. And she was, till her last day, active, no signs of any serious disease. We knew that she had high blood pressure, which could be taken care of correctly-- I don't know why not. Yeah, it was very surprising, her passing away. These are my grandchildren, four of them-- I have five. The youngest here, from the left side, is Robert, presently a student at UCLA. I believe he's either a lawyer-- he will be a lawyer or a journalist. I don't know what he'll decide. This is David, a very smart kid, top student at the University in Davis, medicine he's studying. This is my darling Lisa, my only daughter-- granddaughter. I wanted always to have a daughter, so I got a granddaughter. And then, her brother, Mike. He studies economy, still going to school, and working also. This is certainly somewhere in California when we were on a trip, probably. And the date-- let me see. It must be 1990. Yep. This is my grandson, Jonathan, quite a nice guy. And that seems to be his grandfather on his side, which is me. And this picture had been taken at the wedding of my granddaughter. That was September 4, 1994, at the wedding, which was at the tennis club on Mulholland Drive somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Buch, what is your granddaughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lisa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lisa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lisa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Buch, finally, we noticed that we don't have the pictures of your mother and father. What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Bronia. And my father was Phillip. Bronia [NON-ENGLISH] was her maiden name. And both came from-- my father came from [NON-ENGLISH] and my mother from Lviv, which, today it is Ukraine, I believe. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And much good luck in the future.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you.
